coredata.dc:title: Effects of micro-carbon particles and nano-carbon dots induced structural and mechanical changes of C/C composites prepared by directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI method
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Sheng
Ji, Genshun
Xia, Tingxi
Jia, Jiangang
Zhen, Menghan
Li, Zhou
Hao, Xiangzhong
coredata.dc:description: Commercial carbon felt preforms modified by micro‑carbon particles and nano‑carbon dots were used to fabricate C/C composites by directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI method. Microstructure, fracture morphology, phase constitution and degree of crystallization of the composites were characterized by polarized light microscopy (PLM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD) and Laser confocal Raman microscopy (RMS), respectively. Flexural strength and fracture toughness of the composites was tested by universal electronic testing machine. Results showed that the pyrolysis carbon structure of C/C composites changed from smooth layer to rough layer after the preform was modified by the carbon dots or particles. The degree of graphitization increased from 2.33% of Cf-0/C to 22.09% of Cf-0.05/C; the corresponding fracture mode changed from brittle fracture to pseudo-plastic fracture, and flexural strength slightly decreased.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon/carbon composites
Directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI
Carbon dots
Microstructure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface morphology of the modified carbon fiber
Microstructure analysis of the C/C composites
Characterization of graphene sheets in C/C composites
The formation of high texture pyrolytic carbon matrix
Three-point bending performance and fracture morphology of C/C composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI device.
The TEM images of micro‑carbon particles (a) and nano‑carbon dots (b).
SEM images of carbon fibers surface treated with different sized‑carbon filler solutions (a) zero, (b) 0.05 μm and(c) 5 μm.
Relationship between weight growth rate of C/C composites and deposition time.
Radial density distribution curves of the C/C composites.
PLM diagrams of Cf-0/C (a), Cf-0.05/C (b) and Cf-5/C (c).
SEM morphology of Cf-0/C (a), Cf-0.05/C (b) and Cf-5/C (c).

Effect of carbon dots or micro-carbon particles on the texture of C/C composites (a) Atomic structure of graphite; (b) Physical adsorption and chemisorption of graphene layer; (c) Carbon dots or micro-carbon particles on the surface of carbon fiber induce orderly stacking of basic structural units of pyrolytic carbon; (d) Macroscopic morphology of high-texture pyrolytic carbon induced by carbon dots or micro-carbon particles.
XRD patterns of composites deposited and heat-treated samples.
XRD spectra of heat-treated samples of composites for (002) plane.
Raman spectra of PyC interface after graphitization.
Raman structural parameter R−1 values of different samples.
Flexural strengths, densities and fracture toughness for Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C.
Three-point bending load-displacement curves for Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C.
Fracture morphology of Cf-0/C(a), (d), Cf-0.05/C(b), (e) and Cf-5/C(c), (f).
Relationship between XRD parameters and pyrolytic carbon structure of composite deposited samples and heat-treated samples (HTT = 2000 °C, t = 2 h).

head.ce:title: Effects of micro-carbon particles and nano-carbon dots induced structural and mechanical changes of C/C composites prepared by directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI method

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Commercial carbon felt preforms modified by micro‑carbon particles and nano‑carbon dots were used to fabricate C/C composites by directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI method. Microstructure, fracture morphology, phase constitution and degree of crystallization of the composites were characterized by polarized light microscopy (PLM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD) and Laser confocal Raman microscopy (RMS), respectively. Flexural strength and fracture toughness of the composites was tested by universal electronic testing machine. Results showed that the pyrolysis carbon structure of C/C composites changed from smooth layer to rough layer after the preform was modified by the carbon dots or particles. The degree of graphitization increased from 2.33% of Cf-0/C to 22.09% of Cf-0.05/C; the corresponding fracture mode changed from brittle fracture to pseudo-plastic fracture, and flexural strength slightly decreased.

Commercial carbon felt preforms modified by micro-carbon particles and nano-carbon dots were used for preparation of C/C composites by directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI method. Results showed that the pyrolysis carbon (PyC) structure of the C/C composites changed from smooth layer to rough layer after the preform was modified. The fracture toughness was increased and the flexural strength was slightly decreased.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon fiber or graphite fiber reinforced carbon matrix composites (C/C composites) can be used as high performance structural materials such as disc brakes, engine blade and turbine disk for their excellent friction and wear performance, low density (<2.0 g·cm−3), insensitive to thermal shock, as well as ultra-high temperature mechanical properties [1,2]. Texture of matrix carbon plays an important role for tailoring the properties of C/C composites. Pierson and Lieberman [3,4] divide the morphology of pyrolytic carbon under polarized light into rough layer (RL), smooth layer (SL) and isotropic layer (ISO). Among them, RL pyrolytic carbon has the best comprehensive performance, but the deposition condition range is narrow and difficult to control, so it is not easy to obtain pure rough layer pyrolytic carbon [5].

Micro- and nano-carbon particles have been widely used as fillers for C/C composites due to their diverse structures and superior physical, chemical and mechanical properties. Carbon dots or particles introduced in the preform can increase surface roughness and active sites of the fiber to induce high-texture (RL) pyrolytic carbon and modify the interfacial properties between fiber and matrix. Rajesh Kumar [6] introduced graphite and graphene nanoplatelet into the epoxy composite adhesives and reported a high thermal conductivity enhancement. Dhruv Bansal et al. [7] introduced 1.5 wt% nano-graphite (NGP) filler into matrix of C/C by spraying the NGP during the prepreg formation and obtained a 22% increment in interlaminar shear strength (ILSS), 68% increment in flexural modulus. Shen Qingliang [8] introduced carbon nanotubes (CNTs) into C/C composites by electrophoretic deposition and found that the mass ablation of C/C composites decreased because local thermal damage is significantly relieved and matrix peeling off by high-velocity gas is inhibited. Tai et al. [9] showed an improvements of Young's modulus and tensile strength when single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) were added to phenolic resin.

Carbon dots (CDs) are finding applications in bioimaging [10], surface modification [11], and photocatalysis [12] attribute to their have possess composition-dependent fluorescence, various surface functional groups and fascinating physical and chemical properties [13]. Though many research works on the addition of nano-graphite fillers or graphite into matrix of C/C have been published, there is no systematic study on introducing carbon dots (thus active sites) into carbon fiber preforms to affect the CVI process for preparation of C/C composites. Carbon particles modified carbon fiber may affect the pyrolysis carbon texture, effectively increase the expansion path of the micro-crack, and thus improve the toughness of the C/C composites. In the present work, C/C composites with micro‑carbon particles and nano‑carbon dots modified carbon fiber preforms were prepared by self-made pulsed directional gas flow TG-CVI device that integrates temperature gradient [14], gas flow direction [15] and gas inlet and outlet modes [16]. The effect of micro‑carbon particles and nano‑carbon dots on the microstructure and properties of C/C composites were investigated.

Commercial carbon felt (obtained from Gansu Haoshi Carbon Fiber Co., Ltd) with density of 0.14 g/cm3 and a dimension of Φ85 mm × Φ45 mm × 10 mm is used as the preform, micro-carbon particles (5 μm) and nano‑carbon dots (0.05 μm) (supplied by North China Science and Technology Metal Materials) as the inducers, N-Methyl pyrrolidone (C5H9NO) as the dispersing agent, liquefied gas (PetroChina Lanzhou Petrochemical Company provided) as the precursor, and nitrogen as the diluent gas (Liquefied gas: Nitrogen = 3:1).

Carbon felt preforms were treated with N-methylpyrrolidone solutions containing different carbon fillers, i.e., solutions with zero-, 0.05 μm- and 5 μm-sized carbon filler dispersions. The preform was immersed in the solutions for 2 h under ultrasonic dispersion to sufficiently introduce the carbon dots or particles into the preforms (either trapped in the gap or adhered on the fiber surface). Then, the as treated performs were dried at 180 °C for 10 h to remove the residual solvent of N-methylpyrrolidone. C/C composites deposited from the different solution treated performs were designated as Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C respectively.

The self-made pulsed directional gas flow TG-CVI device is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . Carbon preform was fixed to the uniform temperature zone of a graphite heater. Thermocouples were installed on both sides of the preform. Designate the temperature inside the preform (i.e., the graphite heater) as T1 and the outside temperature as T2. Set T1 to the deposition temperature (1080 °C) and thus formed a radial temperature gradient. During deposition procedure, diluted liquefied gas was decomposed in the high temperature zone to provide carbon sources for the densification of C/C composites. The solenoid valve control pulse intake time was 0.1 s, and the corresponding pulse pressure was −10 to −11 kPa. The overall deposition duration of was 70 h.

The morphologies of the carbon particles were observed by JEM-2010 TEM. Surface morphology of the coated carbon fiber and the C/C composites were observed by a Quanta 450 FEG scanning electron microscope. Densification rate of the composites is calculated by Eq. (1)
(1)
v =
w −
w 0
/ t
where w0 is the initial weight of the preform, w the weight after densification, t the deposition time and ν the densification rate. In order to study the density uniformity, the samples were cut and numbered as shown in Fig. 2 . The volume density of the seven numbered samples was measured through Archimedes' method.

Texture of the pyrolytic carbon was observed with a Zeiss Axio Imager Model A2M polarizing microscope. To examine the concentric pyrocarbon structure, C/C composite samples were treated by a visualization of graphene texture (VGT) method, of which the metallographic samples of the material were slightly oxidized at 600 °C for 20 min, and then the surface morphology was observed by SEM. Phase structure and degree of graphitization of the composites were investigated by Rigaku D/max-2400 X-ray Diffractometer. Laser confocal Raman microscopy (RMS, HORIBA, France) was employed to characterize microstructure of PyC surface.

The flexural strength of the composites was tested by a three-point bending method on an MTS E44.304 electronic universal testing machine with a fixed span of 20 mm and a loading speed of 0.5 mm/min. The sample size for flexural strength tests is 30 mm × 5 mm × 4 mm. At least six specimens were tested under each test condition. The flexural strength was calculated using Eq. (2)
(2)
σ f
= 3 PL / 2
bh 2
where σ f is the flexure strength, MPa; P the maximum breaking load, N; L the span, mm; b the width of the sample, mm; h the height of the sample, mm.

The fracture toughness was tested by using pre-cracked single-edge-notch beam specimens (SENB) with a fixed span of 20 mm. Rectangular crack with a width of 0.25 mm was introduced into the 30 mm × 5 mm × 4 mm sample. At least three specimens were tested under each test condition. The fracture toughness was calculated using Eq. (3)
(3)
K IC
=
3 PL
a ·
10
− 3
2 B
W 2
1.93 − 3.07
a W
+ 14.53
a W
2
− 25.07
a W
3
+ 25.08
a W
4
where P is the peak-load at fracture point, N; L the specimen span, mm; B the specimen-thickness, mm; W the specimen-width, mm and a the crack-length, mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of the modified carbon fiber
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images of micro- carbon particles and nano-carbon dots are illustrated in Fig. 3 . It shows that the sizes of the carbon dots and the micro-carbon particles are about 50 nm and 5 μm respectively. The SEM images of the different treated carbon fiber preforms are shown in Fig. 4 . Pure N-methylpyrrolidone solution treated carbon fiber has a smooth surface [Fig. 4. (a)]. Fig.4 (b) and (c) show images of carbon fibers modified by 50 nm and 5 μm carbon fillers. Carbon dots [see Fig. 4 (b)] and micro-carbon particles [see Fig. 4(c)] are evenly distributed on carbon fiber surfaces apart from small amount of agglomerations. The introduction of carbon dots can essentially increase the active sites on the carbon fibers surface and thereby increase the initial deposition rate of the pyrolytic carbon. On the other hand, the increasing of active sites inhibits nucleation and promotes the growth mechanism for pyrocarbon deposition and thus benefit for the formation of RL carbon.

Fig. 5 shows that the weight growth rate of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C first increases and then decreases. This is because in the initial stage of densification, the internal pores of the preforms were large, and the As/Vr (the ratio of the pore surface area to the pore volume of the porous preform) was relatively low, which is advantageous for the diffusion of hydrocarbon gas. At this stage, the chemical reaction rate was the controlling factor for pyrolytic carbon deposition; the pyrolysis carbon deposition rate was therefore relatively high. With the pyrolysis carbon continued deposition, the internal pores became smaller, and the diffusion mass transfer became the control link of the deposition rate, and the deposition rate decreased gradually.

Density and its distribution are important parameters for evaluating the physical and mechanical performance of C/C composites. Fig. 6 shows the radial density distribution of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C specimens. It is observed that the density outside the specimens is higher than those of the inside. For Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C, density gradients are 0.051 g/cm3, 0.038 and 0.011 g/cm3 between the inner and outer sides, respectively. As the density of the preform increases, the internal pore diameter decreases gradually. When the pore diameter inside the preform is smaller than the mean free path of the gas molecules, Knudsen diffusion gradually replaces Fick diffusion and becomes the main diffusion mode for hydrocarbon gas [17], resulting in difficult diffusion for the gas get into the inside pores of the preforms. In the outer diameter of the preforms, however, the deposition rate still maintains a high level due to the shorter gas diffusion path, and the density outside of sample is therefore slightly higher than inner density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure analysis of the C/C composites

ce:section.ce:para: Polarized microscopy can be used to distinguish different microstructures of pyrolytic carbon in C/C composites [18]. Fig. 7 presents the polarized microstructures of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C. Carbon fibers, which are isotropic, are surrounded by pyrocarbon with certain optical activity, exhibiting an anisotropic characteristic. For Cf-0/C, it is a typical medium texture structure that a layer of bright pyrolytic carbon with chiseled extinction cross and smooth visual effect is formed. Outside of the smooth layer, it turns into a dark-layered tissue with low optical activity, so the microstructure obtained under this condition is SL/DL band structure. Fig. 7 (b) and (c) show the microstructure of Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C, respectively. Both Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C display many growth cones which result in a large number of irregular cross-lights and exhibit high optical activity [19]. It is also shown that the growth cone in Cf-5/C is larger than that in Cf-0.05/C. Moreover, the extinction angle for Cf-0.05/C is 21°and Cf-5/C is 19°, indicating a typical rough layer of pyrolytic carbon. Compared with Cf-0/C, which extinction angle is 14°, the degree of grain orientation for Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C becomes much higher.

The process of depositing pyrolytic carbon with liquefied gas as precursor is very complicated, mainly including pyrolysis of hydrocarbons in high temperature zone, reaction between free radicals, adsorption and desorption. Microstructure of the pyrolytic carbon varies with the deposition environment of the pyrolytic carbon. Fig. 7 (a) shows that the pyrolytic carbon directly deposits on the surface of the carbon fiber, and the pyrolytic carbon structure shows a smooth layer. The short intake time (0.1 s) results in a low pressure and short residence time in the furnace tube. Under this circumstance, homogeneous reactions in the gas are inhibited and small C2 class molecular hydrocarbons tend to directly deposit on the surface of carbon fibers via heterogeneous reactions, thereby the composite exhibits smooth layer pyrolytic carbon. It is noticed that a high-texture pyrolytic carbon appears on the outermost side of the pyrolytic carbon in Fig. 7 (a). With deposition progresses the As/Vr becomes much larger in the later stage of densification. As reaction of reactive species from the gas phase with active sites limits the formation of larger aromatic species, aromatic hydrocarbon species larger enough to be nucleated on the substrate are not formed anymore, resulting in a sudden change from nucleation to the growth mechanism of deposition [20]. Introducing micro‑carbon particles and nano‑carbon dots to carbon fiber surface significantly increases the active sites in the preforms, which is equivalent to increasing the As/Vr. Therefore, a rough layer of pyrolytic carbon is formed from the very initial stage, as shown in Fig. 7 (b) and (c). Due to the different size of the introduced carbon particles on the surface of the carbon fiber, the size of the growth cone is significantly different. The growth cone increases as the carbon dots-induced particles increase.

The carbon particles not only adhere to the surface of carbon fibers but also trap in the pores between fiber bundles, which act as a nucleation core and eventually surround by the pyro‑carbon as indicated by the arrow in the Fig. 7 (c). Since carbon dots can be approximated as spherical at the microscopic level, pyrolytic carbon grown with graphite as a core has good symmetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of graphene sheets in C/C composites
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM microstructure of samples processed by VGT [21] method is shown in Fig. 8 to characterize the graphene sheet structure of pyro‑carbon. It can be seen from Fig. 8(a) that the pyrolytic carbon graphene sheet around the carbon fiber is very thick, indicating that Cf-0/C has more disordered structure regions and the degree of anisotropy is relatively low (medium texture pyrolytic carbon layer). Compared with Cf-0/C, the pyrolytic carbon graphene sheets of Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C are much finer and the texture is clearly visible, indicating that Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C composites have less chaotic layer structure and a higher degree of anisotropy (high-textured pyrolytic carbon layer). In addition, Fig. 8(b) shows more graphene sheets than Fig. 8(c), illustrating the texture of Cf-0.05/C is higher than that of Cf-5/C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation of high texture pyrolytic carbon matrix
ce:section.ce:para:
From the above microstructural results, it can be seen that fixed content of carbon dots with different scales have different effects on the microstructure of composites. Fig. 9 schematically illustrates the carbon dots influencing on the texture of C/C composites. Fig. 9 (a) shows a schematic view of the graphite structure. Graphite is a three-dimensional structure of regularly stacked sp
2 hybrid hexagonal grid layers, having an unsaturated σ bond parallel to the graphene sheet direction and a large π bond perpendicular to the direction of the graphene sheet layer. When the carbon source precursor is pyrolyzed in high temperature zone, linear small molecular hydrocarbons and macromolecular fused aromatic hydrocarbons are formed. The small molecular hydrocarbons and H atoms are easily chemically combined with the unsaturated σ bonds in the graphite to reach a saturated state. Therefore, the growth mechanism of pyrolytic carbon was greatly affected by carbon dots modification. Because of the existence of van der Waals force, the basic structural unit (BSU) forms an ordered stacking layer [21,22], as shown in Fig. 9 (b) and (c). When this physical and chemical adsorption reaches a certain extent, a rough pyrolytic carbon layer is formed [23,24].

The carbon dots or micro-carbon particles are attached to the carbon fiber preform, and the planar macromolecules of the matrix pyrolysis carbon are induced to be oriented radially along the surface of the carbon fiber, which reduces the irregularity of the matrix pyrolysis carbon and wrinkles the orientation. The macroscopically appears to have a distinct growth characteristic, i.e. growth cones. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the Z-axis orientation of the graphite microcrystals in the same cone is the same, and the phase difference in the adjacent cones have a small angle [25]. Thus, the composites should have a strong graphitization ability.

Fig. 10 presents XRD patterns of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C before and after graphitization. It is observed that the diffraction peaks of as-deposited C/C composites show a diffuse package, indicating a disordered structure of pyrolytic carbon. After the graphitization treatment at 2000 °C for 2 h, diffraction peaks for C(002), C(100) and C(004) become sharper and narrower, indicating that the microcrystal structure of pyrolysis carbon changes from turbostratic structure to ideal graphite structure, and the microcrystalline boundary and other lattice defects are removed or disappear by thermal excitation.

Fig. 11 shows the intensity of graphite (002) XRD peaks of the three samples after graphitization. It can be seen that the 2θ values of C(002) plane for Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C are shifted to the right, so the spacing of C(002) surface layer becomes smaller and the degree of graphitization is improved. It also indicates that carbon dots or micro-carbon particles can induce the development of pyrolytic carbon to high texture, and the smaller the carbon particles, the stronger induction effect was.

Table 1 shows the XRD structural parameters of multi-scale carbon dots modified composites before and after graphitization. It shows that the d (002) layer spacing of the different structures in the as-deposited structure is larger than the interstitial layer spacing of 0.3440 nm, and the Lc values are not much different. After graphitization, the C (002) layer spacing is reduced while the Lc value is significantly increased. The graphitization degree for Cf-0.05/C is up to 22.09%; Lc is 14.48 nm, and the number of microcrystalline atomic layers is 42 layers. Graphitization degree for Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C is higher than that of Cf-0/C, indicating that RL pyrolytic carbon is more easily graphitized than SL pyrolytic carbon.

Raman spectral data of the graphitization PyC interface as shown in Fig. 12 . It shows that the amorphous D-band and graphite G-band have good symmetry and the G-band is relatively broad and the D-band is relatively sharp. Fig. 13 illustrates the Raman structure parameter R−1, which reflects the graphitization degree of the C/C composites. The Cf-0.05/C sample has the largest graphitization degree, which result is consistent with the XRD analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-point bending performance and fracture morphology of C/C composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14 presents the flexural strengths, densities and fracture toughness of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C. It can be seen that the flexural strengths of Cf-0/C and Cf-0.05/C are comparable, and are higher than that of Cf-5/C. This is due to the difference in density and pyrolysis carbon structure. The pyrolytic carbon structures for Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C are both RL pyrolytic carbon, but the former strength is stronger than the latter. This is because the material density has a significant influence on the flexural strength. High density means less porosity in the composites, and higher volumetric matrix that can withstand or transfer the applied stress. Moreover, higher density also means smaller internal microcracks and less initial fracture cracks, so the composites with higher density show higher strength. However, the fracture toughness of the Cf-0.05/C is higher than that of Cf-0/C. Because under the same conditions, the fracture toughness of pyrolytic carbon of rough layer will be higher than that of pyrolytic carbon of smooth layer.

Fig. 15 shows typical load-displacement curves of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C during three-point bending test. It is observed that Cf-0/C experiences an elastic deformation mode till a sudden fracture, presenting a catastrophic fracture failure. Whereas Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C, the sudden load reduction corresponds to the crack initiation in the composites, then the load continues to increase (Cf-5/C) or slowly decrease (Cf-0.05/C) till the thorough facture, show more or less a non-catastrophic failure.

Fracture morphology can also reflect the fracture behavior of the material. Fig. 16 shows the fracture morphology of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C. It can be seen that for Cf-0/C, only a few fibers pull out from the matrix and the pullout length is short. The adhesive pyrolysis carbon on the pull-out carbon fiber indicates a strong interfacial bond strength between carbon fiber and matrix. Under the applied load, the matrix first generates complex strain in the pyrolytic carbon, and then external force is transmitted to the carbon fiber through a shear mechanism at the interface. When the crack in the matrix expands to the interface between the fiber and the matrix carbon, the energy reaches the fiber fracture energy, and the stress concentration at the crack tip directly causes the crack. When the carbon fiber is modified with micro‑carbon particles or nano‑carbon dots, the micro‑carbon particles or nano‑carbon dots are attached to the carbon fiber surface, thereby reducing the interfacial bonding force between the matrix carbon and the carbon fiber. As shown in the Fig. 16 (b) and (c), fiber is more significantly extracted than that shown in Fig. 16(a). When the matrix breaks, the crack will expand to the interface between the interlayer. Due to its weak interfacial bonding force, the crack will deflect along the fiber axial direction. The direct fracture of the carbon fiber is avoided, so the composites have pseudoplastic fracture characteristics. Fig. 16(d), (e), and (f) show the enlarged fracture morphology of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C, respectively. It can be seen from the Fig. 16(d) that the pyrolytic carbon fracture is smooth, flush, and the layers are densely bonded, showing the typical smooth layer of pyrolytic carbon. Whereas the pyrolytic carbon fractures in the Fig. 16 (e) and (f) are characterized with gully morphology and lamellar structure, so it belongs to the rough layer pyrolysis carbon. It indicates that the carbon dots induce the directional growth of the pyrolytic carbon layer, but it will cause serious microcracks in the pyrolytic carbon structure and affect the strength of the composites.

Micro‑carbon particles and nano‑carbon dots modified commercial carbon felts were used to prepare C/C composites by directional pulsed gas flow TG-CVI method. The effect of the introduced carbon particles or dots on the texture of pyro‑carbon was discussed. The microstructure of Cf-0/C is SL/DL layer pyrolysis carbon, while the Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C are characterized with rough layer pyrolysis carbon. This effect may be due to the special edge hanging bond of graphite structure, which changes the growth mechanism of pyrolysis carbon. The degree of graphitization increased from 2.33% of Cf-0/C to 22.09% of Cf-0.05/C; the corresponding fracture model changed from brittle fracture to pseudo-plastic fracture. The density of the three C/C composites, which has a decrease tendency along the serial of Cf-0/C, Cf-0.05/C and Cf-5/C, is responsible for the decrease of the flexural strength.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Sheng Zhang:Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft.Genshun Ji:Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing.Tingxi Xia:Resources, Visualization.Jiangang Jia:Software, Formal analysis.Menghan Zhen:Validation, Data curation.Zhou Li:Validation, Data curation.Xiangzhong Hao:Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declared that we have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Gansu Provincial science and technology major project (1602GKDD012) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 51461029).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of pre-fatigue on bending behavior of 2.5D C/C composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, S. Yu, Y. Li, Q. Wu, Z. Li, T. Xiao, L. Liu, X. Guo, Effect of pre-fatigue on bending behavior of 2.5D C/C composites, Mater. Sci. Eng, A. 682 (2017) 290-295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of diamond graphitization on the microstructure and performance of micro-diamond modified C/C composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Chen, X. Yang, Q.Z. Huang, C.Q. Fang, A.H. Shi, R.R. Liu, Influence of diamond graphitization on the microstructure and performance of micro-diamond modified C/C composites, Diam. Relat. Mater. 95 (2019) 99-108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of gas phase conditions on resultant matrix pyrocarbons in carbon/carbon composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Lieberman, H.O. Pierson, Effect of gas phase conditions on resultant matrix pyrocarbons in carbon/carbon composites, Carbon. 12 (1974) 233-241.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The chemical vapor deposition of carbon on carbon fibers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.O Pierson, M.L. Lieberman, The chemical vapor deposition of carbon on carbon fibers, Carbon. 13 (1975) 159-166.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Damage tolerance of carbon-carbon composites in aerospace application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Chowdhury, H. Sehitoglu, R. Rateick, Damage tolerance of carbon-carbon composites in aerospace application, Carbon. 126 (2018) 382-393.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study on epoxy resin-based thermal adhesive filled with hybrid expanded graphite and graphene nanoplatelet

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Kumar, S. Mohanty, S.K. Nayak, Study on epoxy resin-based thermal adhesive filled with hybrid expanded graphite and graphene nanoplatelet. SN Applied Sciences. (2019) 1:180.
D. Bansal, S. Pillay, U. Vaidya, Nanographite-reinforced carbon/carbon composites, Carbon. 55 (2013) 233-244.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic deposition of carbon nanotubes for improved ablation resistance of carbon/carbon composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.L. Shen, H.J. Li, F.L. Zhao, Q. Song, Q.G. Fu, Electrophoretic deposition of carbon nanotubes for improved ablation resistance of carbon/carbon composites, Corros. Sci. 132 (2018) 204-213.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.H. Tai, M.K. Yeh, T.H. Peng, Experimental study and theoretical analysis on the mechanical properties of SWNTs/phenolic composites, Composites Part B. 39 (2008) 926-932.

Pengju G. Luo, Sushant Sahu, Sheng-Tao Yang, Sumit K. Sonkar, Jinping Wang, Haifang Wang, Gregory E. LeCroy, Li Cao and Ya-Ping Sun, Carbon “quantum” dots for optical bioimaging, J. Mater. Chem. B. 1 (2013) 2116–2127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and GO modified WO3 nanosheets combination as an effective visible photo catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ghulam Sughra Jamila, Shamaila Sajjad, Sajjad Ahmed Khan Leghari, and Mingce Long, Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots and GO modified WO3 nanosheets combination as an effective visible photo catalyst, J. Hazard. Mater. 382 (2020) 121087.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: N-doped carbon dots/CdS hybrid photocatalyst that responds to visible/near-infrared light irradiation for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Weilong Shi, Feng Guo, Mingyang Li, Yu Shi and Yubin Tang, N-doped carbon dots/CdS hybrid photocatalyst that responds to visible/near-infrared light irradiation for enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production, Sep. Purif. Technol. 212 (2019) 142–149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smart utilization of carbon dots in semiconductor photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Huijun Yu, Run Shi, Yufei Zhao, Geoffrey I. N. Waterhouse, Li-Zhu Wu, Chen-Ho Tung, and Tierui Zhang, Smart Utilization of Carbon Dots in Semiconductor Photocatalysis, Adv. Mater. 28(2016), 9454-9477.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of infiltration conditions on the densification behavior of carbon/carbon composites prepared by a directional-flow thermal gradient CVI process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.H. Tang, D.N. Qu, J. Xiong, Z.Q. Zou, Effects of infiltration conditions on the densification behavior of carbon/carbon composites prepared by a directional-flow thermal gradient CVI process, Carbon. 41 (2003) 2703-2710.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S Lewis, W.J. Lackey S. Vaidyaraman, Model for prediction of matrix microstructure for carbon/carbon composites prepared by forced flow-thermal gradient CVI, Carbon. 35 (1997) 103-112.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. L. Dong, K.J. Hüttinger, Consideration of reaction mechanisms leading to pyrolytic carbon of different texture, Carbon. 40 (2002) 2515-2528.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.G. Zhang, Z.J. Hu, K.J. Hüttinger, Chemical vapor infiltration of carbon fiber felt optimization of densification and carbon microstructure, Carbon. 40 (2002) 2529-2545.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.L. Dong, K.J. Hüttinger, Consideration of reaction mechanisms leading to pyrolytic carbon of different textures, Carbon. 40 (2002) 2515-2528.
